Finally, the implicit meaning that this rule must always hold globally is represented by the 'G' (globally) operator. Therefore, the complete LTL formula is G(routeChange -> (abort & requestPermission)).

The consequence 'the autonomous system should stop and seek approval of the new plan' breaks down into two parts: 'stop' and 'seek approval of the new plan'. 'Stop' is represented by the negation of 'proceed' (i.e., !proceed), which means the robot ceases to continue its current plan. 'Seek approval of the new plan' directly maps to 'requestPermission'. These two actions must happen together, so they are combined with an AND operator: '!proceed & requestPermission'. The word 'should' implies that this combined action must eventually occur after the route change, so we use the 'F' (finally) operator: 'F(!proceed & requestPermission)'. The overall structure 'If A, then B' is an implication 'A -> B'.
